note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence pauses. Never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; only <break/> tags and the italic director notes were stripped, and the block-header lines dropped. Block windows are MEASURED onsets: FFT cross-correlation of each sNN.mp3 against the joined 8 kHz-mono master. The nominal Sigma(ffprobe audio + 0.4) formula overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (228.284s) by 0.60s (> 0.3 tolerance) because ffprobe's mp3 durations overstate decoded sample counts; the cross-correlated onsets land exactly at Sigma(decoded_audio + 0.4) with a clean 0.400s gap after every block (master 8 kHz decode 228.240s). Last block endS set to the ffprobe master duration 228.284s per the audioDurationS rule.

phrases.text: Open I-T and Support.
Down the sidebar, past the support tools, sits a small group called Audit.
Three pages.
Between them they answer almost every who-did-this question a client will ever send you.
Open the Audit Log.
This is the master feed.
Every meaningful event on the platform lands here in one running list, newest first,
with a dashboard on top to read the shape of it before you dig.
Start with the four tiles.
Events today, events this week, how many different people were active,
and how many events tripped a critical flag.
One glance tells you whether today looks normal or busy.
To the left, the yearly trend.
Twelve months of logged activity in a single line, ticking up.
It's context, not an alarm: is the platform getting busier, or holding steady.
Next, the split by action.
Most of what gets logged is edits and new records.
Logins are a chunk.
Deletions are the thin slice at the bottom,
and on a support ticket, that thin slice is usually the one you came for.
Beside it, severity.
Nearly everything is normal.
A little is a warning.
A sliver is critical, and that sliver is the same three events the top tile just flagged.
Two views of one number.
Now the list itself.
Read a row left to right and it tells a whole story:
when, who, what they did, in which module, to which record, at which location,
and the I-P address it came from.
There are Export and New Record buttons up here.
Leave them. You're reading, not changing.
Find the deleted event.
Here it is: a payment record, removed, tied to a name, a location, and an I-P.
That's the power of this page.
When something goes missing, you don't guess who touched it.
You read the row.
Move to User Activity.
Where the Audit Log tracks events, this page tracks people at the door.
Who's signing in, how often, and who keeps bouncing off the login screen.
Its tiles lead with the sign-in picture.
Active users, logins today, average session length,
and the one you'll reach for most: failed logins.
When a cashier says they're locked out, that number is where you start.
The table fills in the rest.
Each person, their role, when they last got in, how many times this month, from where,
and whether the account is active.
It answers a quiet but common question:
is this user actually signing in, and as what.
Last page. Change History.
The Audit Log tells you a record was updated.
This one tells you exactly which field moved, and what it was before.
The tiles count the churn.
How many changes today, how many this week,
and how the load splits between configuration and everyday data.
It's a pulse on how much is being edited under the hood.
But the columns are the payoff.
Alongside the usual when and who, this page names the exact field that changed
and lays the old value and the new value side by side.
No other audit page does that.
Picture a membership price.
The plan's example is a monthly rate moving from
twenty-nine ninety-nine to thirty-four ninety-nine.
One row, before on one side, after on the other.
When a client swears their price jumped, this is the receipt.
So, three pages, three questions.
What happened, and from what machine? That's the Audit Log.
Who's getting in, and who isn't? User Activity.
Who changed which field, and to what? Change History.
Match the ticket to the page, and the answer is already waiting.
